90-233
JR

LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

MARIN COUNTY
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

May 21, 1990


Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Follow-up on Request for Formal Advice
    re Wilson for Governor and Yes on 115

Dear Mr. Rothman:

     This letter is to follow-up on my letter to you of May 11,
1990, concerning the dilemma in which the Yes on 115 Committee
and the Pete Wilson for Governor Committee found themselves
because of the conflicting requirements of Proposition 105 and
proposed Regulation 18538.

     We understand that you could not respond in writing before
May 19, 1990, the closing date for the second pre-election
campaign report. As you now know, this report governs all statewide
initiative disclosure required by Proposition 105 in connection
with the primary election. This letter is to set forth facts
that have occurred May 11, 1990, and the decisions taken by
both of these Committees.

     Before May 19, 1990, the Pete Wilson for Governor Committee
purchased a substantial television buy to support Yes on 115.
This was done with the knowledge and coordination of the Yes on
115 Committee. The Wilson Committee sent a notice
to the treasurer of Yes on 115 informing him of the size of the
buy. Consequently, the treasurer is now aware that the Pete
Wilson for Governor Committee is the largest major funding source
to Yes on 115, as defined by Government Code §84507(b).

     Because you originally told me that you believed that the
Proposition 105 disclaimers would be required by the Yes on 115
Committee for a largest contributor who is a candidate controlled
committee (even though such disclosure conflicts with the spirit
of Proposition 73 and currently with proposed Regulation 18538),
and because you confirmed after receipt of my May 11, 1990 letter
that you did not believe the staff would change its opinion, we
informed both the Yes on 115 Committee and the Wilson Committee
that such disclosure is necessary on all Yes on 115
advertisements.

May 21, 1990
Page 2

We still believe that the Commission can determine for
either of the two reasons set forth in our May 11, 1990 letter
that such candidate committee disclosure is not required under
Proposition 105. However, this is now a subject for future
discussion, and we hope it will be looked at carefully after the
immediacy of other pending questions that relate to the primary
are answered.

We appreciated the fast response to our verbal inquiries and
your verbal confirmations after receipt of our May 11, 1990
letter. Early this summer, our firm will try to think of pending
Proposition 105 questions and ask them early enough that there
will be fewer last-minute questions.

Very truly yours,

[handwritten signature]

Thomas W. Hiltachk

TWH:dc

LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

May 11, 1990

Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Formal Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Rothman:

This letter is a follow-up to our conversation regarding the application of Proposition 105 (Govt. Code §§ 84501-84514) to Pete Wilson For Governor - 1990 and Crime Victims For Justice, Yes on 115. As you know, this firm is legal counsel to both committees.

Pete Wilson For Governor - 1990 is a candidate controlled committee formed to support Senator Wilson's campaign for governor. Crime Victims For Justice is a candidate controlled committee formed to support Proposition 115 on the June primary ballot. Senator Wilson is one of the controlling candidates of this committee.

As we discussed, I am seeking formal advice regarding the application of Proposition 105 to the following situations:

Pete Wilson For Governor - 1990 will produce and air a television commercial at its own expense (from money collected in amounts under Proposition 73 limits) in which Pete Wilson explains his support of Proposition 115 and urges voters to support it. The Wilson Committee agrees that the commercial also benefits Pete Wilson's candidacy. You confirmed that since Pete Wilson For Governor is paying all costs of the advertisement, Proposition 105 does not require disclosure of Crime Victims For Justice's major funding sources on the Wilson ads.

John Rothman, Esq.
May 11, 1990
Page 2


Question 1: Does Proposition 105 require Pete Wilson For
Governor to examine its campaign reports to determine if any
major funding source category applies?

Pete Wilson For Governor will report the costs of the
spots on Schedule E of its Form 420 Report. Once Pete Wilson For
Governor airs the commercial described above, using the campaign
disclosure rules of the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended,
it has made either an independent expenditure to support
Proposition 115 or an in-kind contribution to Crime Victims For
Justice. But since Pete Wilson is a controlling candidate, we
assume the value of the commercial is reportable as an in-kind
contribution. But if this is true, does this non-monetary
contribution make Pete Wilson For Governor a major funding source
of Crime Victims For Justice under section 84507(b)?

Senator Wilson would prefer not to be compelled by
Proposition 105 to be disclosed as a major funding source of
Proposition 115, which will pay for its advertisements with money
that is permitted to be collected in amounts not limited by
Proposition 73. As you know, at the Commission meeting in
October 1989, Senator Wilson publicly opposed the use of "soft
money" not received within the limits by ballot measure
committees for promotion of a candidate's campaign for election.
Pete Wilson believes the Proposition 105 disclosure, if required,
would benefit his gubernatorial campaign in a manner inconsistent
with proposed regulation 18538 and the intent of Proposition 73.
In fact, a Crime Victims' ad that announces that Pete Wilson For
Governor provided major funding for the spot could be an illegal
non-monetary contribution by Crime Victims to Pete Wilson For
Governor. We therefore request response to the following
additional questions:

Question 2: Is a candidate's controlled committee, primarily
formed to support his or her candidacy, a "person" within the
meaning of Government Code section 84507(b)?

As we discussed, it is doubtful that the proponent of
Proposition 105 intended to require disclosure of a candidate's
committee as a "major funding source." We ask you to answer this
question in the negative.

John Rothman, Esq.
May 11, 1990
Page 3


Question 3: Assuming a candidate's primarily-formed controlled
committee is a "person" as defined in Government Code sections
84507(b), how is Proposition 105 to be reconciled with
Proposition 73?

     It is Senator Wilson's position that the public policy
requiring expenditures benefitting a candidacy to be made from
funds collected in amounts under Proposition 73 limits should
prevail over Proposition 105. Where the policies underlying the
two measures conflict in this manner, perhaps "generic"
disclosure such as "major funding provided by a(statewide)
candidate committee" (or something similar) would serve to
effectuate both acts.

     The primary election is approaching very quickly. You
have informally told me that Pete Wilson For Governor must review
its own campaign reports in response to question number one and
that Crime Victims must disclose Pete Wilson For Governor as a
major funding source if the commercial is an in-kind contribution
received prior to the last pre-election report in response to
question number two. In view of the limited time before the
primary, please notify me immediately if the information provided
in this letter has changed your position. We would appreciate
formal written advice on all three questions as soon as possible.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [illegible]

                                        THOMAS W. HILTACHCK

TWH/bg
6411.01


________________

     ¹ This conflict between Proposition 105 and 73 exists for
other candidates as well. For example, the press has reported
that the Van de Kamp for Governor committee has made
contributions in excess of $100,000 to the qualification efforts
of two and maybe three initiatives that he has chosen to make as
centerpieces of his candidacy for Governor, likely making Van de
Kamp for Governor a major funding source of these measures.
